formulas.context: Recalling Equations 6.1, 6.2b, and 6.3b, the response in trait value, $ R_z = \overline{z}' - \overline{z} $, becomes $$ \begin{aligned}R_{z}&=\sum_{i}q_{i}^{\prime}\overline{z}_{i}-\sum_{i}q_{i}z_{i}\\&=\left(\sum_{i}q_{i}^{\prime}z_{i}-\sum_{i}q_{i}z_{i}\right)+\left(\sum_{i}q_{i}^{\prime}\overline{z}_{i}-\sum_{i}q_{i}^{\prime}z_{i}\right)\\&=\sum_{i}\left(q_{i}^{\prime}-q_{i}\right)z_{i}+\sum_{i}q_{i}^{\prime}\left(\overline{z}_{i}-z_{i}\right)\\&=\sum_{i}\Delta q_{i}z_{i}+\sum_{i}q_{i}^{\prime}\overline{\delta}_{i}\\ \end{aligned} $$ (6.4)

source.subsection: The Life and Times of George Price

formulas.context: Both these terms can be expressed in a more transparent form. For the first term, $$ \begin{align*}\sum_{i}\Delta q_{i}z_{i}&=\sum_{i}\left(w_{i}q_{i}-q_{i}\right)z_{i}=\sum_{i}w_{i}z_{i}q_{i}-\sum z_{i}q_{i}=E(w_{i}z_{i})-E(z)\cdot1\\&=E(w_{i}z_{i})-E(z)\cdot E(w)\\&=\sigma(w_{i},z_{i})\end{align*} $$ (6.5a)

source.subsection: The Life and Times of George Price

formulas.context: Note that to obtain this result, we use the identity $ E(w) = 1 $. For the second term, $$ \sum_{i}q_{i}^{\prime}\overline{\delta}_{i}=\sum_{i}q_{i}w_{i}\overline{\delta}_{i}=E(w_{i}\overline{\delta}_{i}) $$ (6.5b)

source.subsection: The Life and Times of George Price

formulas.context: Substituting Equations 6.5a and 6.5b into Equation 6.4 yields the more traditional form of the Price equation, $$ R_{z}=\overline{z}^{\prime}-\overline{z}=\sigma(w_{i},z_{i})+E(w_{i}\overline{\delta}_{i}) $$ (6.6)

source.subsection: The Life and Times of George Price

formulas.context: Several equivalent expressions for the Price equation are useful in that they emphasize different features. For example, using the definition of a covariance $$ E(w_{i}\overline{\delta}_{i})=\sigma(w_{i},\overline{\delta}_{i})+E(w_{i})\cdot E(\overline{\delta}_{i})=\sigma(w_{i},\overline{\delta}_{i})+E(\overline{\delta}_{i}) $$ (6.7a) and substituting this result into Equation 6.6 yields $$ R_{z}=\sigma(w_{i},z_{i})+\sigma(w_{i},\overline{\delta}_{i})+E(\overline{\delta}_{i}) $$ (6.7b)

Biologically, this last assumption implies that the relative fitness of an individual is uncorrelated with the residual error, $ e_A

Substituting this expression for w in Equation 6.25a yields $$ R_{z}=\sigma(w,A_{z})=\sigma(A_{w}+e_{w},A_{z})=\sigma(A_{w},A_{z})+\sigma(e_{w},A_{z}) $$ (6.25c) showing that while the 1966 version is more general, the two versions (Equations 6.24a and 6.25a) are equal when $ \sigma(e_w, A_z) = 0 $.

If we write $ w = A_w + e_w $, Equation 6.28 becomes $$ \begin{aligned}\Delta\overline{A}_{z}&=\sigma(A_{w}+e_{w},A_{z})+\sigma(w,\overline{\delta})+E(\overline{\delta})\\&=\sigma(A_{w},A_{z})+\sigma(e_{w},A_{z})+\sigma(w,\overline{\delta})+E(\overline{\delta})\end{aligned} $$ (6.29) which recovers Robertson’s 1968 version (Equation 6.25a), provided that the last three terms are zero (or else are very small relative to the first term).

One is conditional covariance, $ \sigma(x, y \mid z) $, defined as the covariance conditioned on the value of z: $$ \sigma(x,y\mid z)=E\Biggl[\biggl(x-E(x\mid z)\biggr)\biggl(y-E(y\mid z)\biggr)\mid z\Biggr] $$ (6.33a)

The linear regression of x on z can be written as $$ x=\mu_{x}+\beta_{x|z}z+e_{x}=\mu_{x}+\left(\frac{\sigma(x,z)}{\sigma_{z}^{2}}\right)z+e_{x} $$ implying that $$ e_{x}=x-\left(\mu_{x}+\beta_{x|z}z\right)=x-\mu_{x}-\left(\frac{\sigma(x,z)}{\sigma_{z}^{2}}\right)z $$ (6.33c) with a similar expression for $ e_{y} $. source.subsection: Partial Covariance and the Spurious Response to Selection

formulas.context: yields $$ \sigma(x,y||z)=\sigma(e_{x},e_{y})=\sigma\left[x-\left(\frac{\sigma(x,z)}{\sigma_{z}^{2}}\right)z,y-\left(\frac{\sigma(y,z)}{\sigma_{z}^{2}}\right)z\right] $$ which, by expansion, leads to $$ \begin{aligned}\sigma(x,y||z)&=\sigma(x,y)-\left(\frac{\sigma(x,z)}{\sigma_{z}^{2}}\right)\sigma(y,z)-\left(\frac{\sigma(y,z)}{\sigma_{z}^{2}}\right)\sigma(x,z)+\left(\frac{\sigma(y,z)}{\sigma_{z}^{2}}\right)\left(\frac{\sigma(x,z)}{\sigma_{z}^{2}}\right)\sigma_{z}^{2}\\&=\sigma(x,y)-\frac{\sigma(x,z)\sigma(y,z)}{\sigma_{z}^{2}}\quad\end{aligned} $$ (6.33d)

Applying Equation 6.33d by taking $ x = w $ and $ y = \overline{z} $ yields $$ \begin{align*}\sigma(w,\overline{z}||z)=\sigma(w,\overline{z})-\frac{\sigma(\overline{z},z)}{\sigma_{\overline{z}}^2}\sigma(w,z)=\sigma(w,\overline{z})-\beta_{\overline{z}|z}\sigma(w,z)\end{align*} $$ (6.33e)

formulas.context: Finally, while $ \bar{\delta}_{i} $ is often considered to be the change from parent to offspring in the absence of selection, this is not strictly correct. Formally, it has two components, $$ \overline{\delta}_{i}=\overline{z}_{i}-z_{i}=(\overline{z}_{i}^{\circ}+\delta_{i}^{\circ})-z_{i}=\delta_{i}^{\circ}+(\overline{z}_{i}^{\circ}-z_{i}) $$ (6.37)

source.subsection: Heywood's Decomposition of Response

Expressed in terms of a regression using parents before selection ( $ \beta_{\overline{z}^{o},z} $), substituting Equations 6.35–6.38 into Equation 6.32 yields $$ R=\beta_{\overline{{z}}^{\circ}|z}S+\sigma(w,\overline{{z}}^{\circ}||z)+\sigma(w,\delta^{\circ})+E(\delta^{\circ})+E(\overline{{z}}^{\circ}-z) $$ (6.39)
